sources_audited.note: 403s to plain fetch; read in full via browser. Every figure used matches exactly: total employment 1,236,740; Construction & Extraction 41.25%; Installation/Maintenance/Repair 29.51%; Office & Admin Support 12.16% of employment and ~8.7% of wage bill (150,410 x $47,980 over 1,236,740 x $66,850); Business & Financial 4.29%; Sales 2.52%; Management 5.62%.

O*NET-based methodology and the direction (physical/mechanical work least exposed, analytical/information work most exposed) confirmed on the page. The specific claim that construction/extraction and installation/maintenance/repair occupations 'cluster in the least-exposed quartile' is a reasonable reading of Pew's framework but is not directly stated in the main text (detailed occupational lists sit in an appendix).
Page confirms survey of 1,000+ contractors: ~60% familiar with AI, >70% see it as relevant, only ~12% have embedded AI into workflows.
Page states FSM adoption 29% for firms under 100 employees vs 76%+ for 1,000+ employee organizations, exactly as used.

Tracker confirms Sila Services and Apex Service Partners as active repeat acquirers, but verifies 20-21 active plumbing roll-up platforms, not the '27+ active PE-backed consolidation platforms' stated in the s5 rationale ('more than two dozen' in the memo). Identity supported; count overstated relative to this source.

S&P Global hard-blocks both plain fetch and browser (Access Denied). The cited figure was corroborated via secondary coverage of the same Capstone Partners midyear analysis: 32 PE add-on transactions in HVAC services through June 9, 2025, up 88% from 17 in the prior-year period.
Page confirms robust HVAC M&A through H1 2025 (30 deals tabulated Jan-Jun 2025) and multiples 'north of 10x EBITDA' for high-revenue-visibility, service-heavy businesses.

Page supports the claims used: average small-business owner nearly 60, more than half over 55, plumbing ownership skewing older, and limited succession options. Note the age datum is Census-derived for small businesses generally, applied to plumbing by the source.
Page states 76% of home-services companies performing critical/rare services are independents and 70% of plumbing-repair market share is held by independents, as cited.
Page confirms US HVAC market $65.20B (2025) to $82.72B (2030), 4.88% CAGR, as used in the d5 bridge.

403s to plain fetch; read via browser. Confirms sharp 2025 declines but the packet's 'central A/C and heat-pump unit shipments were down roughly 20% year-to-date through September 2025' is accurate only for central A/C alone (-20.2%); heat pumps were down 6.2% and the combined total ~14%. Article attributes the decline to the A2L transition and R-454B shortage; the 25C pull-forward linkage is the researcher's inference (25C expiry itself is sourced to S17). The overstatement is conservative for d5 (it deflates demand further).

Page supports growing service share of revenue and service-over-install margin superiority (HVAC service 30-45% GM vs install 18-28%; plumbing service 35-55% vs 20-30%). It does NOT contain the '~39%' or 'a third to two-fifths of service revenue' maintenance-agreement share attributed to it in the packet's supports field and used in the q rationale and lens (checked twice, including targeted search).

Page genuinely compares flat-rate vs time-and-materials billing and says flat rate reduces uncertainty and books more appointments, but contains no statistics at all — the '92%' / 'nine in ten homeowners' preference figure attributed to it does not appear on the page.

Page gives HVAC CPL benchmarks of $34 (branded), $72 (Performance Max), $149 (non-branded), consistent with the packet's '$40-150 range' used as competitive-intensity evidence (category-level CPLs run higher, up to $231).

Page supports 'vast majority of business owners do not have a succession plan' and over half of owner-employers being 55+, but the specific 92%-closure/5%-sale/3%-transfer split cited in the s5 caveat was not found in the page text (possibly in a linked infographic not renderable as text). The researcher already flags this baseline as a broad proxy and adjusts away from it.

JS-rendered page read via browser. Confirms state-by-state licensing with most states requiring a state-level HVAC contractor license, a few (e.g., Colorado, Illinois, Indiana) with no state requirement but local licensing, plus nationwide EPA Section 608 certification — exactly as used for o.
Page confirms Section 25C credit applies only to equipment placed in service on or before December 31, 2025 (terminated early by OBBBA), as used for the d5 demand headwind.

Full PDF text extracted and searched. Neither cited figure appears anywhere in the document: no '225,000' HVAC technician shortfall, no '41%' of the construction workforce retiring by 2031, no mention of 2031 at all. The report does support a general skilled-trades shortage ('Labor shortages are also high among electricians, plumbers, and HVAC technicians, with over half of surveyed builders reporting shortages') and an aging-workforce dynamic (Baby Boomers 14.2% of the construction labor force, implying substantial near-term retirement). Direction supported; both specific magnitudes are misattributed to this source and repeated in the memo narrative.

caveats: S18 misattribution: the packet and memo attribute a '225,000-technician HVAC shortfall within five years' and '41% of the construction workforce projected to retire by 2031' to the HBI Fall 2025 report, which contains neither figure. The report does support the qualitative shortage claim (over half of surveyed builders report shortages of electricians, plumbers, and HVAC technicians). Not material: the shortage is used as directional support in d5 and o, whose bases remain defensible on the surviving evidence, D is 10.0 at base regardless, the base tier (STRUCTURAL_OUT) is driven entirely by H, and the action (VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS) follows from MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness.

S13 does not contain the '92%'/'nine in ten homeowners prefer flat rate' figure used in the q rationale and lens; the cited page has no statistics at all, only a qualitative flat-rate vs T&M comparison. The qualitative direction (flat-rate favored, books more jobs) is on the page. Correcting this would not move C enough to matter: even q at its low (0.40) leaves C at 8.0 and changes neither tier nor action.

S12 does not contain the 'roughly a third to two-fifths (~39%) of service revenue from maintenance agreements' figure attributed to it, though it does support the service-over-install margin gap and growing service share that carry the q and e reasoning.

S5's tracker verifies 20-21 active plumbing roll-up platforms, not the '27+ active PE-backed consolidation platforms' in the s5 rationale ('more than two dozen' in the memo). The platform identities and repeat-acquirer behavior are confirmed, and s5's base (0.32) does not depend on the precise count.

S11 nuance: the ~20% YTD 2025 shipment decline is accurate for central A/C alone; heat pumps were down 6.2% and the combined figure ~14%. The article attributes the drop to the A2L transition and refrigerant shortage, not the 25C pull-forward (that linkage is the researcher's inference). The overstatement runs in the conservative direction for d5.

S15's specific 92%/5%/3% closure/sale/transfer split was not found in the cited page's text (majority-no-succession-plan is supported); the researcher already discloses this baseline as a broad proxy and adjusts away from it.

Several q- and lens-supporting sources (S12, S13, S14) are vendor/SEO marketing content of low evidentiary grade, a weakness the packet itself partially discloses; the score-driving H-factor sources (S1, S2, S3, S4) are the strongest in the packet and fully or substantially verified.
S6 is hard-blocked to automated access (S&P Global); its 32-deals/+88% figure was corroborated through secondary coverage of the same Capstone Partners analysis rather than the cited page itself.
summary: Mechanics pass cleanly (identity, score, and memo all reproduce). All 18 primitive-cited sources were reopened; 17 were reachable (S6 is bot-blocked but its figure was corroborated via secondary coverage of the same underlying Capstone analysis). The evidence chain that actually drives the result is solid: the base tier STRUCTURAL_OUT comes from H = 0.79, and H's inputs are the packet's best-verified sources — the BLS OEWS occupation mix matches to the decimal, and the Pew, ServiceTitan, and FSM-adoption figures check out. Evidence states are honest (both PROXY bridges name real source populations, transformations, and error directions; nothing unknown is scored as zero), the lens narrowing is disclosed, coherent, and does not favor the record (the score is out anyway on H, which the lens cannot inflate), ranges are honest rather than theatrical, and the driver/weakness narrative follows directly from the registered evidence. The audit did find a cluster of misattributed decorative statistics — most notably the 225,000-technician shortfall and 41%-retirement figures attributed to an HBI report that contains neither, plus the 92% flat-rate preference, the ~39% maintenance-agreement share, and a 27+ platform count that its cited tracker puts at 20-21. Each was tested for materiality and none passes: they sit in q, d5, o, and s5, whose factors are at or near their caps, while the base tier is pinned by H and the action is pinned by MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, so correcting or downgrading any of them changes neither the base tier nor the action category. The record is honest, useful, and publishable with the caveats recorded here.
